I think I agree with this change, but for a different reason. `self.sequence` calls and caches the result of `to_list()` when in reality it should not be necessary to evaluate the entire list just to do `head_option`. `head` is written such that it does not need to do this so calling it gains the benefit of that.

```
In [1]: from functional import seq

In [2]: a = seq.range(10)

In [4]: def f(x):
   ...:     print('eval', x)
   ...:     return x
   ...:

In [5]: a.map(f).head()
eval 0
Out[5]: 0

In [6]: a = seq.range(10)

In [7]: a.map(f).head_option()
eval 0
eval 1
eval 2
eval 3
eval 4
eval 5
eval 6
eval 7
eval 8
eval 9
Out[7]: 0
```

Having state on the trait for this seems like the wrong way to do this. E.g. any on_change callbacks for the traitlet will be able to modify it, because they'll be called while it's 'unlocked'. Likewise, if there's more than one thread that might try to change it, some changes that should raise an error could randomly succeed.

I'm not convinced this belongs in traitlets at all, but if it does, I think the design should be fundamentally different. For instance, a main interface using properties that is always read only, and a different 'back door' interface that can be used to update the stored value.

Yeah it would be `Boto3/1.7.19 Python/2.7.12 Linux/4.4.0-122-generic Botocore/1.10.19` assuming `boto3` is being used. To be more specific, it is the `user_agent` value attached to the client and session (depending on if using `boto3` or `botocore` client/session) i.e.
```python
>>> import boto3
>>> c = boto3.client('s3')
>>> c.meta.config.user_agent
'Boto3/1.7.21 Python/2.7.10 Darwin/15.6.0 Botocore/1.10.21'
>>> import botocore.session
>>> s = botocore.session.Session()
>>> s.user_agent()
'Botocore/1.10.21 Python/2.7.10 Darwin/15.6.0'
```
I think along with the consistency argument, the other big problem (and probably more concerning) is that we are breaking the contract for `user_agent` if we are not using the value attached to the client/session when setting the user agent. The `user_agent` is documented more here:
https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/dbfd6ee157c61222a4090d6191ce2937f12d184f/botocore/session.py#L478-L499
and here:
https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/dbfd6ee157c61222a4090d6191ce2937f12d184f/botocore/config.py#L31-L32

But the contract essentially implies that whatever value `user_agent` is, the SDK will use that value when injecting the `User-Agent` header. So, the way I see it right now is that we are breaking that contract by using the default header set by `requests`, but by setting it to something other than what is attached the client/session would still be breaking the `user_agent` contract. 

Let us know if that makes sense or you have any thoughts.

If you check the end of `tests.py` file (starting with line#1555), you notice, we first create an instance of `LogOutput` class, `log_output`, and we initialize the `function` attribute with the name of the api command/method that will go under test.

For example, in the above test case `test_make_bucket_default_region`, `log_output.function` is set to `make_bucket(bucket_name, location)` by default.

Because some tests, like `test_make_bucket_default_region`, depend on some other `helper` api commands, it would be misleading to report a helper command ,`bucket_exists`, failure with the name of the api command, `make_bucket`, under test. That's why, I  set `log_output.function` to 'bucket_exists(bucket_name)'` before calling `bucket_exists` api/helper command. The same with `remove_bucket` helper api command. This way, the name of the failed api command  will be reflected in the generated Json format output in the `function` field.

Imagine we have a factor whose values are 50% identical. If we run the tear sheet with a quantiles value different than 2 we'll get a "ValueError: Bin edges must be unique"  excepion because the identical values would split in more than one quantile and this would lead to bid edges with the same value. 

With this PR we would avoid the exception but the quantiles that contain (fully or partially) the identical values are arbitrary, as we could swap securities with identical factor values between quantiles. The consequence of having generated arbitrary quantiles is that we can only trust the alphalens statistics calculated for the quantiles that do not contain the identical values (the quantiles that are not arbitrary) and we have to look at the quantiles with identical values as just one of the multiple possibilities the identical values can be split into.

So, I can see the limits of this PR but there are the cases where the identical values are only a very small percentage of the total amount (still an exception can be raised if the a bin edge falls in the middle of the identical value group) and in that case the skew would be small.

`fetch_metric_descriptor` and `fetch_resource_descriptor` raise `NotFound` if the thing does not exist.

Both kinds of methods can be useful — raising `NotFound` or returning `None` — but it's rarely worth providing both. ```python
```python
>>> from dateutil.parser import parse
>>> parse('20050931')
Traceback: [...]
ValueError: day is out of range for month
```

This means the parser would fail for the unambiguous date '20050931', which is unambiguously May 20, 931. This condition can only be true if we've "missed" a lower bound for a given point in `dataPoints`.  By advancing past `dataPoints[i]` without assigning anything to its `lower` property we're setting things up for an error down the line when we compute `fillArea`, right?  Ditto for the upper bound logic below.

There are various ways we could attempt to handle those gaps (setting the missing bound to `scalar` or omitting that data point entirely) but I think it would be perhaps least surprising to just emit an error and refuse to plot a series if we can't find matching-step lower and upper bounds for each data point.

If we do that, I think there might be a better way to structure this algorithm so that it basically just iterates over the data points and for each one, scans ahead in both bound arrays until it finds bounds at matching steps (and if it doesn't find them, it reports an error).  Alternatively we could be even stricter and not permit more bounds than data points, either (while that case would not force a plotting error, it's unlikely to come up in practice and might still represent incorrect usage).  That would make the algorithm super simple since you'd just walk all three lists in sync and assert that the steps match.

Actually, no. In Python 2 the function's descriptor makes an unbound `instancemethod`, which enforces that the first argument must be of the type that the method was created on. In Python 3 an unbound method, accessed directly on the class, is just directly accessing the function. In this case I didn't want to create a real `Session` object (even mocking with a Spec didn't work because the `config` property is added dynamically), so I had to unwrap the actual function so I could call it.

The trick is to find `protoc`. For a system with multiple protobuf versions installed, user decides which one to use by setting the `PATH` to pickup the right protoc, and library would be under `../lib`. This is picked up from Caffe2 cmake file and the goal is to be consistent with Caffe2's behavior so that they end up picking the same protobuf.
